About the comment, "No one's developing an unhealthy attachment to duolingo?" The first time I accidentally missed a day and lost my multi-month streak, back in like 2019 when missing a single day was all it took, I was already in a bad place mentally and physically, and Duo sending me a sad, teary-eyed Duo did trigger a significant sense of guilt and failure that lasted a couple months and prevented me from trying another lesson. It's not DuoLingo's fault that I was dealing with some at-the-time undiagnosed conditions, but I think of it whenever I see the Duo skeleton on somebody else's phone.

I also periodically experience attacks of intense paranoia, and happened to be hovering on the edge of one a couple months ago when I received a DuoLingo notification that said something along the lines of, "We'll let your family go after you do your lesson." I was planning to do my lesson anyway, and I was able to recognize that it was a joke, but it made me feel very uncomfortable and question if my family might actually be in danger and I just didn't know it. Does that make logical sense? No. Does mental illness care if something makes logical sense? Also no. Thankfully, I'm in a place where I can laugh about it and talk about it, but not everyone who experiences these sorts of episodes can say the same thing, and I suspect I'm not the only person who's experienced a moment like that.

As for the second part of your comment, I think there's a difference between a critique and telling someone that they must be lying about a diagnosis because they speak about their condition differently than you do. Two people with the same condition can have very different experiences and very different approaches to dealing with it and describing it. It may be as simple as having different senses of humor or knowing different vocabulary for things, coming from different cultural backgrounds, or even being in a different age group. The way I talk about mental and physical illness in my 40's is very different from how my friends who are in their 20's and 30's talk about it, and that's okay.

It doesn_¢____t matter if you understand what_¢____s being said or not. The goal is to get used to the sounds and rhythm of the language. 

If there are words you don_¢____t know that comes up a lot, look them up and memorize them. That way you_¢____ll piece together a list of the most common words and bit by bit you_¢____ll find that you understand more and more. It_¢____s incredibly satisfying once it gets going

A long time ago in the Burgh of Pitt a fledgling business started making languages courses. This was very labor intensive so many languages were created by teams of volunteers. Many of the smaller more obscure language courses were built that way. 

As the company grew it became clear that it would be more appropriate for course development to be done by staff. (You will notice that the quality varies considerably between the volunteer courses.) With limited resources they had to put a priority on languages that had the most demand. 

Of course it takes a lot of man hours to write lessons in all the language combinations so it would take years for courses to get expanded. They realized they could speed up this process with AI and thus were able to release over 100 long awaited courses in 2025. 

When they went AI first they knew that there would be bumps along the road. We've seen some of those posted here, where a corrected answer has nothing to do with the question or a wrong conjugation has been used. In some languages they've seen rather boring stories created by AI. So the AI efforts are a work in progress. They will either work out the kinks to resolve these issues or they will falter. But they do seem to want to solve these problems, so fingers crossed....

As for the other topics, I believe they realized that they could use a similar process for teaching other subjects. They decided to pick subjects that are both useful and that appeal to a broad audience. And thus we have math, music and chess.
